12.title: Ch 1: Relations and Functions
12.content:
◆ TYPES OF RELATIONS: 
1. Reflexive: (a, a) ∈ R for all a ∈ A.
2. Symmetric: If (a, b) ∈ R, then (b, a) ∈ R.
3. Transitive: If (a, b) ∈ R and (b, c) ∈ R, then (a, c) ∈ R.
◆ EQUIVALENCE RELATION: A relation that is Reflexive, Symmetric, and Transitive simultaneously.
◆ FUNCTIONS: 
- One-to-One (Injective): Every element in domain has a unique image.
- Onto (Surjective): Range equals Codomain.
- Bijective: Both One-to-One and Onto. Only bijective functions have inverses.
◆ COMPOSITION: (gof)(x) = g(f(x)). The domain of 'g' must include the range of 'f'.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in cryptography for secure data transmission and in defining relationships in complex social network databases.
◆ TIP: To check for equivalence, always test all three properties step-by-step.

12.title: Ch 2: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
12.content:
◆ DEFINITION: Inverse of trig functions within restricted domains to ensure they are bijective.
◆ PRINCIPAL VALUE BRANCHES:
- sin⁻¹x: [-π/2, π/2]
- cos⁻¹x: [0, π]
- tan⁻¹x: (-π/2, π/2)
◆ KEY FORMULAS: 
- sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x = π/2
- tan⁻¹x + tan⁻¹y = tan⁻¹[(x+y)/(1-xy)]
- 2tan⁻¹x = sin⁻¹[2x/(1+x²)] = cos⁻¹[(1-x²)/(1+x²)].
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in engineering to find unknown angles in structures and in navigation systems for determining bearings.
◆ LOGIC: The output of an inverse trigonometric function is always an ANGLE.
◆ TIP: Always ensure the calculated angle lies within the specified Principal Value Branch range.

12.title: Ch 3: Matrices
12.content:
◆ ORDER: Rows × Columns. A matrix with m rows and n columns is of order m × n.
◆ TYPES: Row, Column, Square, Diagonal, Identity (I), and Zero matrix.
◆ OPERATIONS: 
- Addition/Subtraction: Possible only if orders are identical.
- Multiplication: Number of columns in 1st = Number of rows in 2nd. (AB ≠ BA).
◆ TRANSPOSE (A'): Interchanging rows and columns. (AB)' = B'A'.
◆ SYMMETRIC: A = A'. SKEW-SYMMETRIC: A = -A'.
◆ INVERSE: Exists only for square matrices where |A| ≠ 0.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in 3D computer graphics for transformations (scaling, rotation) and in solving large systems of linear equations in physics.
◆ TIP: All diagonal elements of a skew-symmetric matrix are always 0.

12.title: Ch 4: Determinants
12.content:
◆ EVALUATION: Expanding along rows or columns. For a 2x2: ad - bc.
◆ PROPERTIES: 
- If two rows/columns are proportional or identical, determinant is 0.
- Interchanging any two rows/columns flips the sign of the determinant.
◆ ADJOINT: Transpose of the cofactor matrix. A(adj A) = |A|I.
◆ INVERSE FORMULA: A⁻¹ = (1/|A|) * adj A.
◆ APPLICATIONS: Solving linear equations using Cramer's Rule or Matrix Inverse method (X = A⁻¹B).
◆ AREA OF TRIANGLE: ½ |x1(y2-y3) + x2(y3-y1) + x3(y1-y2)|.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in structural engineering to test the stability of building designs and in digital image processing.
◆ TIP: Use properties like adding rows to create maximum zeros before expanding.

12.title: Ch 5: Continuity and Differentiability
12.content:
◆ CONTINUITY: A function is continuous at x = c if LHL = RHL = f(c). No jumps or breaks in the graph.
◆ DIFFERENTIABILITY: Existence of a unique tangent at a point. Differentiability implies continuity.
◆ DIFFERENTIATION RULES: 
- Chain Rule: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) * g'(x).
- Logarithmic: Used when variable is in power (y = xˣ).
- Parametric: dy/dx = (dy/dt) / (dx/dt).
◆ SECOND ORDER DERIVATIVE: Differentiating dy/dx again (d²y/dx²).
◆ MEAN VALUE THEOREM: Rolle’s Theorem and Lagrange’s Mean Value Theorem (LMVT).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used to find the speed of a vehicle at a specific moment (speedometer) and in predicting the spread of viruses.
◆ TIP: If a function has a 'sharp turn' or corner (like |x|), it is not differentiable at that point.

12.title: Ch 6: Application of Derivatives (AOD)
12.content:
◆ RATE OF CHANGE: dy/dx represents how 'y' changes with respect to 'x'.
◆ INCREASING/DECREASING: f'(x) > 0 means increasing; f'(x) < 0 means decreasing.
◆ TANGENTS & NORMALS: Slope of tangent m = dy/dx. Slope of normal = -1/m.
◆ MAXIMA & MINIMA: 
- First Derivative Test: Sign change of f'(x).
- Second Derivative Test: f''(x) < 0 (Maxima), f''(x) > 0 (Minima).
◆ APPROXIMATIONS: f(x + Δx) ≈ f(x) + f'(x)Δx.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Architects use it to maximize the floor area of a building within a fixed perimeter and in rocket science to find peak altitude.
◆ TIP: In word problems, the quantity to be maximized/minimized should be expressed in terms of a single variable.

12.title: Ch 7: Integrals
12.content:
◆ INDEFINITE: The process of finding the antiderivative. ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C.
◆ METHODS: 
1. Substitution: Putting t = f(x).
2. Partial Fractions: For rational expressions.
3. By Parts (ILATE): ∫uv dx = u∫v - ∫(u'∫v)dx.
◆ DEFINITE: Area under a curve within limits. ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a).
◆ PROPERTIES: King's Property: ∫[0,a] f(x)dx = ∫[0,a] f(a-x)dx.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in physics to calculate the total work done by a variable force and in geography to measure the area of irregular land.
◆ TIP: Don't forget the integration constant 'C' for indefinite integrals.

12.title: Ch 8: Application of Integrals
12.content:
◆ AREA UNDER CURVE: Finding the area bounded by simple curves like circles, parabolas, and ellipses.
◆ AREA BETWEEN TWO CURVES: ∫ [f(x) - g(x)] dx between the intersection points.
◆ STRATEGY: 
1. Sketch the graph accurately.
2. Find intersection points (limits).
3. Use vertical (y dx) or horizontal (x dy) strips.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Engineers use this to calculate the volume of fuel in a tank and in medicine to measure blood flow rate.
◆ LOGIC: Integration is the continuous sum of infinitely many thin rectangles.
◆ TIP: Area is a magnitude; if the integral comes out negative, take the absolute value.

12.title: Ch 9: Differential Equations
12.content:
◆ ORDER: Highest order derivative present. DEGREE: Power of the highest order derivative.
◆ SOLUTIONS: 
1. Variable Separable: Grouping y terms with dy and x terms with dx.
2. Homogeneous: Substitute y = vx.
3. Linear Equations: dy/dx + Py = Q. Integrating Factor (IF) = e^∫Pdx.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used to model population growth, radioactive decay, and cooling of objects (Newton’s Law of Cooling).
◆ GENERAL vs PARTICULAR: General solution has a constant 'C', Particular solution finds 'C' using given values.
◆ TIP: Degree is only defined if the differential equation is a polynomial in its derivatives.

12.title: Ch 10: Vector Algebra
12.content:
◆ MAGNITUDE: |a| = √(x² + y² + z²). Unit vector â = a/|a|.
◆ SCALAR PRODUCT (DOT): a.b = |a||b| cosθ. Result is a number.
◆ VECTOR PRODUCT (CROSS): a × b = |a||b| sinθ n̂. Result is a perpendicular vector.
◆ PROJECTION: Projection of vector 'a' on 'b' is (a.b)/|b|.
◆ DIRECTION COSINES: l, m, n satisfying l² + m² + n² = 1.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in physics to calculate work (dot product) and torque (cross product). Also essential in video game physics for character movement.
◆ TIP: If the dot product is zero, the vectors are perpendicular.

12.title: Ch 11: Three Dimensional Geometry
12.content:
◆ DIRECTION RATIOS (DRs): Any numbers proportional to Direction Cosines.
◆ LINE EQUATION: Vector form r = a + λb. Cartesian form (x-x1)/a = (y-y1)/b = (z-z1)/c.
◆ SHORTEST DISTANCE: Formula for distance between skew lines (lines that are not parallel and don't intersect).
◆ PLANE: Vector form r.n = d. Cartesian form ax + by + cz + d = 0.
◆ ANGLE: Finding the angle between two lines or a line and a plane using cosine/sine formulas.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in air traffic control to prevent plane collisions and in designing complex architectural structures.
◆ TIP: The numbers (a, b, c) in the plane equation are the direction ratios of the normal to that plane.

12.title: Ch 12: Linear Programming (LPP)
12.content:
◆ OBJECTIVE FUNCTION: Z = ax + by (The value we want to maximize or minimize).
◆ CONSTRAINTS: Linear inequalities that restrict the variables.
◆ FEASIBLE REGION: The common area on a graph where all inequalities overlap.
◆ CORNER POINT METHOD: Checking the value of Z at every vertex of the feasible region.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Businesses use it to maximize profit or minimize production costs while dealing with limited labor and materials.
◆ LOGIC: The optimal solution always occurs at the boundaries/corners of the region.
◆ TIP: If the feasible region is unbounded, an extra check is needed for the maximum/minimum value.

12.title: Ch 13: Probability
12.content:
◆ CONDITIONAL PROBABILITY: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B). Probability of A given that B has occurred.
◆ MULTIPLICATION THEOREM: P(A ∩ B) = P(A) * P(B|A).
◆ BAYES' THEOREM: A way to find the probability of a cause given the observed effect.
◆ RANDOM VARIABLE & DISTRIBUTION: Table of all possible outcomes and their probabilities where ΣP(x) = 1.
◆ BINOMIAL DISTRIBUTION: Used for 'n' independent trials with only two outcomes (Success/Failure).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in AI for spam filtering, in insurance to calculate premium rates, and in genetics to predict traits.
◆ TIP: Use a tree diagram for multi-stage probability problems to avoid confusion.

10.title: Ch 1: Real Numbers
10.content:
◆ CONCEPT: Understanding numbers from integers to irrationals. Euclid’s Division Lemma: a = bq + r (0 ≤ r < b).
◆ FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM: Every composite number is a unique product of primes. HCF × LCM = Product of numbers.
◆ IRRATIONALITY: Proof of √2, √3, √5 using the contradiction method.
◆ DECIMAL EXPANSION: Terminating if denominator is 2ⁿ5ᵐ, otherwise non-terminating repeating.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in digital clock synchronization and cryptography (coding-decoding) for secure messages.
◆ TIP: For 'Finding the largest possible size' problems, always calculate HCF.

10.title: Ch 2: Polynomials
10.content:
◆ ZEROES: Points where the graph crosses the X-axis. Geometrical interpretation of linear, quadratic, and cubic.
◆ RELATION: α + β = -b/a, αβ = c/a. For cubic: α+β+γ = -b/a, αβ+βγ+γα = c/a, αβγ = -d/a.
◆ DIVISION ALGORITHM: p(x) = g(x)q(x) + r(x). Finding all zeroes when two are given.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in trajectory calculation (path of a ball) and architectural arch designs (parabolas).
◆ TIP: The number of zeroes is always equal to the degree of the polynomial.

10.title: Ch 3: Linear Equations in Two Variables
10.content:
◆ CONDITIONS: a1/a2 ≠ b1/b2 (Unique), a1/a2 = b1/b2 ≠ c1/c2 (No solution), a1/a2 = b1/b2 = c1/c2 (Infinite).
◆ METHODS: Substitution, Elimination, and Cross-multiplication. Consistency and Inconsistency of systems.
◆ WORD PROBLEMS: Speed-Time-Distance, Age, and Downstream/Upstream (u+v and u-v).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used by economists to find 'Break-even points' where cost equals revenue.
◆ TIP: Elimination method is the most efficient for complex calculations.

10.title: Ch 4: Quadratic Equations
10.content:
◆ STANDARD FORM: ax² + bx + c = 0. Quadratic Formula: x = [-b ± √(b²-4ac)] / 2a.
◆ DISCRIMINANT (D): b² - 4ac. D > 0 (Distinct), D = 0 (Equal), D < 0 (No Real Roots).
◆ METHODS: Factorization (splitting middle term), completing the square, and using D.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in calculating the area of plots, signal processing, and lens designs in optics.
◆ TIP: If D is a perfect square, the roots will always be rational numbers.

10.title: Ch 5: Arithmetic Progressions (AP)
10.content:
◆ FORMULAS: aₙ = a + (n-1)d. Sₙ = n/2 [2a + (n-1)d]. Common difference 'd' is constant.
◆ SELECTION: 3 terms (a-d, a, a+d). 4 terms (a-3d, a-d, a+d, a+3d).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in calculating loan EMI payments, pyramid structures, and biological patterns (growth).
◆ TIP: If n-th term is linear in n, it's an AP. If Sₙ is quadratic in n, it's an AP.

10.title: Ch 6: Triangles
10.content:
◆ SIMILARITY: SSS, AAA, SAS. Basic Proportionality Theorem (BPT) and its converse.
◆ THEOREMS: Ratio of areas = (Side1/Side2)². Pythagoras Theorem (H² = P² + B²).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in 'Thales' shadow method to find the height of pyramids/towers without climbing.
◆ TIP: All circles and all squares are always similar to each other.

10.title: Ch 7: Coordinate Geometry
10.content:
◆ DISTANCE: √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²]. Section: [(m₁x₂+m₂x₁)/(m₁+m₂), (m₁y₂+m₂y₁)/(m₁+m₂)].
◆ AREA: ½ |x₁(y₂-y₃) + x₂(y₃-y₁) + x₃(y₁-y₂)|. If Area = 0, points are collinear.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Basis of GPS navigation, Google Maps, and pixel placement in computer screens.
◆ TIP: Centroid of a triangle is the average of its x and y coordinates.

10.title: Ch 8: Introduction to Trigonometry
10.content:
◆ RATIOS: sin=P/H, cos=B/H, tan=P/B. Identities: sin²θ+cos²θ=1, 1+tan²θ=sec²θ, 1+cot²θ=cosec²θ.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in satellite communications, sound wave analysis, and oceanography to measure tides.
◆ LOGIC: Every trigonometric ratio can be converted to any other ratio using identity formulas.
◆ TIP: Use the 'Super Hexagon' trick to remember all identities easily.

10.title: Ch 9: Some Applications of Trigonometry
10.content:
◆ CONCEPTS: Angle of Elevation (looking up), Angle of Depression (looking down). Height = Base × tanθ.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used by pilots for landing approach angles and by foresters to measure tree heights.
◆ STEP: Always draw the ground line (horizontal) and the line of sight (hypotenuse) first.
◆ TIP: For 45°, Height always equals the Distance from the base.

10.title: Ch 10: Circles
10.content:
◆ PROPERTIES: Tangent is perpendicular to the radius. Tangents from an external point are equal in length.
◆ THEOREM: Angle between two tangents is supplementary to the angle between the radii.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in manufacturing gears, pulleys, and calculating the rotation speed of wheels.
◆ TIP: A tangent never enters the interior of a circle; it only kisses the surface.

10.title: Ch 11: Areas Related to Circles
10.content:
◆ FORMULAS: Sector = (θ/360)πr². Segment = Sector Area - Triangle Area. Arc = (θ/360)2πr.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Design of circular gardens, calculating the coverage of a car wiper or a water sprinkler.
◆ LOGIC: A circle is just an infinite-sided polygon with a constant radius.
◆ TIP: Area of a circle is 4 times the area of its quadrant.

10.title: Ch 12: Surface Areas and Volumes
10.content:
◆ SHAPES: Combination of solids. Frustum of a cone (Bucket). Volume of sphere = 4/3 πr³.
◆ CONVERSION: When one shape is melted to another, Volume remains constant (V1 = V2).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in packaging industries to find the minimum cardboard needed (TSA) to hold a liquid (Vol).
◆ TIP: In TSA of combined solids, don't add the areas of faces that are hidden.

10.title: Ch 13: Statistics
10.content:
◆ MEASURES: Mean (Σfx/Σf), Median (l+[(n/2-cf)/f]h), Mode (l+[(f1-f0)/(2f1-f0-f2)]h).
◆ EMPIRICAL: 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean. Ogives (Less than/More than graphs).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used by insurance companies for risk calculation and by retailers for inventory management.
◆ TIP: Median is the only measure that doesn't get affected much by extreme outliers.

10.title: Ch 14: Probability
10.content:
◆ BASICS: P(E) = Favorable/Total. P(E) + P(not E) = 1. Range is 0 to 1.
◆ EXPERIMENTS: Dice (6 outcomes), Coins (2ⁿ outcomes), Cards (52 total).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in weather forecasting (80% chance of rain) and the stock market for risk analysis.
◆ TIP: 'At least' means that number or greater; 'At most' means that number or smaller.

11.title: Ch 1: Sets
11.content:
◆ DEFINITION: Well-defined collection. Roster form vs Set-builder form. Subsets (2ⁿ).
◆ OPERATIONS: Union (A∪B), Intersection (A∩B), Difference (A-B). Venn Diagrams.
◆ DE-MORGAN: (A∪B)' = A'∩B'. n(A∪B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A∩B).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in database management (SQL joins) and grouping users in social media algorithms.
◆ TIP: Power set of an empty set is not empty; it contains {∅}.

11.title: Ch 2: Relations and Functions
11.content:
◆ CONCEPTS: Cartesian Product A×B. Relation is a subset of A×B. Function: Each input → Unique output.
◆ TYPES: Modulus |x|, Signum x/|x|, Greatest Integer [x], Identity, Constant.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Every computer program is a function where you give input and get a specific output.
◆ TIP: Use the Vertical Line Test on a graph; if it crosses twice, it's not a function.

11.title: Ch 3: Trigonometric Functions
11.content:
◆ TRIGONOMETRY TABLE (Full Values):
  - 0°: sin=0, cos=1, tan=0, cosec=ND, sec=1, cot=ND
  - 30°: sin=1/2, cos=√3/2, tan=1/√3, cosec=2, sec=2/√3, cot=√3
  - 45°: sin=1/√2, cos=1/√2, tan=1, cosec=√2, sec=√2, cot=1
  - 60°: sin=√3/2, cos=1/2, tan=√3, cosec=2/√3, sec=2, cot=1/√3
  - 90°: sin=1, cos=0, tan=ND, cosec=1, sec=ND, cot=0
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in oscillating systems like guitar strings, pendulums, and AC current waves.
◆ FORMULAS: sin(A+B), cos(A+B), tan(A+B), 2x and 3x formulas.
◆ TIP: 'ASTC' (After To School To College) helps remember positive signs in quadrants.

11.title: Ch 4: Complex Numbers & Quadratic Equations
11.content:
◆ IOTA: i = √-1. z = a + ib. Polar Form: r(cosθ + i sinθ). Modulus |z| = √(a²+b²).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Electrical engineers use complex numbers to calculate 'Impedance' in AC circuits.
◆ QUADRATIC: Solving ax²+bx+c=0 for negative discriminants using 'i'.
◆ TIP: Always rationalize a complex number in the denominator by multiplying with its conjugate.

11.title: Ch 5: Linear Inequalities
11.content:
◆ RULES: Shading the feasible region. Flipping signs when multiplying by negative values.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in logistics to find 'Minimum shipping time' or 'Maximum load' a truck can carry.
◆ GRAPH: Solution is an area, not a point. Broken line for <, >, solid line for ≤, ≥.
◆ TIP: Always test (0,0) to determine which side of the line to shade.

11.title: Ch 6: Permutations and Combinations
11.content:
◆ FORMULAS: ⁿPᵣ = n!/(n-r)! (Arrangement). ⁿCᵣ = n!/r!(n-r)! (Selection).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Creating secure passwords, arranging books, and picking teams in sports.
◆ LOGIC: Permutation is for 'Order', Combination is for 'Choice'.
◆ TIP: ⁿCᵣ = ⁿCₙ₋ᵣ. This makes calculating ¹⁰⁰C₉₈ as easy as ¹⁰⁰C₂.

11.title: Ch 7: Binomial Theorem
11.content:
◆ EXPANSION: (a+b)ⁿ = ΣⁿCᵣ aⁿ⁻ʳ bʳ. Total terms = n+1.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in probability theory and for calculating interest rates in banking models.
◆ MIDDLE TERM: One if n is even, two if n is odd. Pascal’s Triangle for coefficients.
◆ TIP: The sum of powers of 'a' and 'b' in every term always equals n.

11.title: Ch 8: Sequences and Series
11.content:
◆ AP & GP: aₙ = arⁿ⁻¹. Sₙ = a(rⁿ-1)/(r-1). Infinite GP: S∞ = a/(1-r) for |r|<1.
◆ MEANS: Arithmetic Mean (AM) and Geometric Mean (GM). Relation: AM ≥ GM.
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in calculating population growth, interest rates, and radioactive decay.
◆ TIP: If a sequence grows very fast, try checking for a common ratio (GP).

11.title: Ch 9: Straight Lines
11.content:
◆ SLOPE (m): tanθ. Point-slope: y-y₁=m(x-x₁). Slope-intercept: y=mx+c. Intercept: x/a + y/b = 1.
◆ DISTANCE: From point to line = |ax₁+by₁+c|/√(a²+b²).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Architects use line equations for roof slopes and civil engineers for road gradients.
◆ TIP: The angle between two lines depends only on their slopes (m1 and m2).

11.title: Ch 10: Conic Sections
11.content:
◆ SHAPES: Circle, Parabola, Ellipse, Hyperbola. Focus, directrix, and eccentricity (e).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Elliptical orbits of planets, parabolic headlight reflectors, and hyperbolic cooling towers.
◆ LOGIC: Every conic is a section of a cone cut at a specific angle.
◆ TIP: Eccentricity 'e' is the ratio of distance from focus to distance from directrix.

11.title: Ch 11: 3D Geometry
11.content:
◆ SPACE: (x, y, z). Distance = √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)² + (z₂-z₁)²]. Octants (8 total).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in 3D gaming, CAD design, and air traffic control to track plane height/location.
◆ SECTION: Finding a point between two coordinates in 3D space.
◆ TIP: If a point is in the XY plane, its Z-coordinate is always 0.

11.title: Ch 12: Limits and Derivatives
11.content:
◆ LIMITS: lim(x→0) sinx/x = 1. Derivatives: f'(x) using the First Principle.
◆ RULES: Product (uv)' = u'v + uv', Quotient Rule, Power Rule (nxⁿ⁻¹).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in chemistry to find 'Instantaneous' reaction rates and in physics for velocity.
◆ TIP: Limit represents what a function *looks* like it's going to hit, even if it's undefined there.

11.title: Ch 13: Statistics
11.content:
◆ DISPERSION: Mean Deviation, Variance (σ²), and Standard Deviation (σ).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Scientists use SD to see how much their experimental results vary from the average.
◆ LOGIC: Variance measures the 'spread' or 'risk' in a set of data.
◆ TIP: Standard deviation is the square root of Variance and has the same units as the data.

11.title: Ch 14: Probability
11.content:
◆ ADVANCED: Mutually Exclusive, Independent, and Exhaustive events.
◆ FORMULA: P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A∩B). If independent, P(A∩B) = P(A) × P(B).
◆ PRACTICAL/LAB: Used in genetics to predict hereditary traits and in insurance for calculating risk.
◆ TIP: 'Independent' means one event happening does not change the chance of the other.

8.title: Ch 1: Rational Numbers
8.content:
◆ DEFINITION: Numbers expressed as p/q (q ≠ 0).
◆ CLOSURE: Addition, subtraction, and multiplication of rationals result in a rational number.
◆ COMMUTATIVITY: a + b = b + a. This holds for addition and multiplication but fails for subtraction (5-3 ≠ 3-5) and division.
◆ ASSOCIATIVITY: (a+b)+c = a+(b+c). Helps in regrouping large sums.
◆ DISTRIBUTIVITY: a(b+c) = ab + ac. Crucial for solving brackets.
◆ IDENTITIES: 0 is the additive identity. 1 is the multiplicative identity.
◆ INVERSES: Additive inverse of 'x' is '-x'. Multiplicative inverse (reciprocal) of 'a/b' is 'b/a'.
◆ BETWEEN NUMBERS: There are infinite rational numbers between any two given rational numbers.
◆ CALCULATION TIP: Always convert mixed fractions to improper fractions first.
◆ EXAMPLE: Find 5 numbers between 1/2 and 2/3 by making the denominator 30.
◆ PROPERTY: Rational numbers are closed under division only if we exclude zero.

8.title: Ch 2: Linear Equations in One Variable
8.content:
◆ CONCEPT: Equations with one variable having a maximum power of 1.
◆ EQUALITY: An equation is like a balance scale; LHS must always equal RHS.
◆ TRANSPOSING: Shifting terms. Rules: + becomes -, - becomes +, × becomes ÷, and ÷ becomes ×.
◆ CROSS-MULTIPLICATION: Used when both sides are fractions: (a/b) = (c/d) → ad = bc.
◆ SOLVING STEPS:
1. Remove brackets if any.
2. Bring variable terms (x) to one side.
3. Bring constants to the other side.
4. Solve to isolate x.
◆ WORD PROBLEMS: Key phrases to note:
- 'Sum' means (+), 'Difference' means (-).
- 'Times' or 'Product' means (×).
- 'Is' or 'Result' means (=).
◆ AGE PROBLEMS: If current age is x, age 'n' years ago was (x-n) and after 'n' years will be (x+n).
◆ DIGIT PROBLEMS: A two-digit number is represented as 10x + y.

8.title: Ch 3: Understanding Quadrilaterals
8.content:
◆ CURVES: Simple closed curves made of line segments are Polygons.
◆ CLASSIFICATION: Triangle (3), Quadrilateral (4), Pentagon (5), Hexagon (6), Heptagon (7), Octagon (8).
◆ DIAGONALS: A line segment connecting non-consecutive vertices.
◆ CONVEX vs CONCAVE: Convex polygons have no portions of diagonals in their exteriors.
◆ ANGLE SUM PROPERTY:
- Interior angles sum = (n-2) × 180°.
- Exterior angles sum = Always 360° for any polygon.
◆ TYPES OF QUADRILATERALS:
1. PARALLELOGRAM: Opposite sides are equal and parallel. Diagonals bisect each other.
2. RHOMBUS: A parallelogram where all sides are equal. Diagonals are perpendicular bisectors.
3. RECTANGLE: A parallelogram with right angles. Diagonals are equal.
4. SQUARE: All sides equal, all angles 90°. Diagonals are equal and perpendicular bisectors.
5. KITE: Two pairs of equal adjacent sides.
6. TRAPEZIUM: One pair of parallel sides.

8.title: Ch 4: Data Handling
8.content:
◆ DATA COLLECTION: Gathering information for specific purposes.
◆ TALLY MARKS: Groups of 5 (|||| with a slash) to count frequency easily.
◆ GROUPING: Used for large data. Class intervals (e.g., 0-10, 10-20).
◆ TERMS: Lower limit (start of interval), Upper limit (end of interval).
◆ HISTOGRAM: A bar graph for grouped data where bars have no gaps.
◆ PIE CHART: A circle graph showing components of a whole.
- Central Angle = (Value / Total) × 360°.
◆ PROBABILITY: Measure of uncertainty.
- Formula: P(E) = Number of outcomes / Total possible outcomes.
- If a coin is tossed, P(Heads) = 1/2.
- If a die is thrown, P(getting 6) = 1/6.
- Probability is always between 0 and 1.

8.title: Ch 5: Squares and Square Roots
8.content:
◆ SQUARE NUMBER: A number multiplied by itself (n²).
◆ PROPERTIES:
- Ending digits: Must be 0, 1, 4, 5, 6, or 9.
- Numbers ending in 2, 3, 7, or 8 are NEVER perfect squares.
- Squares of even numbers are even; odd are odd.
◆ PYTHAGOREAN TRIPLET: (2m, m²-1, m²+1).
◆ SQUARE ROOT (√): The inverse operation of squaring.
◆ METHODS:
1. REPEATED SUBTRACTION: Subtracting 1, 3, 5, 7... until zero.
2. PRIME FACTORISATION: Making pairs of prime factors.
3. LONG DIVISION: Best for large numbers and decimals.
◆ ESTIMATION: Guessing square roots of non-perfect squares by finding nearest perfect squares.
◆ FACT: The sum of the first 'n' odd numbers is n².

8.title: Ch 6: Cubes and Cube Roots
8.content:
◆ CUBE: A number raised to power 3 (n³).
◆ PROPERTIES:
- Cubes of numbers ending in 1 end in 1.
- Cubes of numbers ending in 2 end in 8, and vice versa.
- Cubes of numbers ending in 3 end in 7, and vice versa.
◆ HARDY-RAMANUJAN NUMBER: 1729 is the smallest number expressible as the sum of two cubes in two different ways (1³+12³ and 9³+10³).
◆ CUBE ROOT (∛): Inverse of cube. Finding 'x' such that x³ = y.
◆ METHOD: Prime Factorisation is the most reliable way (Make groups of 3 identical factors).
◆ ESTIMATION METHOD: Split digits from right in groups of 3 and find digits based on unit place properties.
◆ VOLUME CONNECTION: The volume of a cube with side 's' is s³.

8.title: Ch 7: Comparing Quantities
8.content:
◆ RATIO: Comparison of two quantities of the same unit.
◆ PERCENTAGE: A ratio where the second quantity is 100.
◆ PROFIT AND LOSS:
- Profit = SP - CP | Loss = CP - SP.
- Profit % = (Profit/CP) × 100.
◆ DISCOUNT: Reduction in Marked Price (MP). SP = MP - Discount.
◆ SALES TAX/GST: Added to the bill amount.
◆ COMPOUND INTEREST (CI): Interest on previous interest.
- Amount (A) = P(1 + R/100)ⁿ.
- CI = A - P.
◆ POPULATION PROBLEMS: Use CI formula for growth or depreciation (R is negative for depreciation).

8.title: Ch 8: Algebraic Expressions and Identities
8.content:
◆ TERMS: Parts of an expression separated by + or -.
◆ COEFFICIENT: The numerical factor of a term.
◆ TYPES: Monomial (1 term), Binomial (2), Trinomial (3), Polynomial (many).
◆ LIKE TERMS: Terms having identical algebraic factors (e.g., 2x and 5x).
◆ MULTIPLICATION: Term by term using the distributive law.
◆ STANDARD IDENTITIES:
1. (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
2. (a-b)² = a² - 2ab + b²
3. (a+b)(a-b) = a² - b²
4. (x+a)(x+b) = x² + (a+b)x + ab
◆ USAGE: These identities help in mental calculation of squares like 101² = (100+1)².

8.title: Ch 9: Mensuration
8.content:
◆ AREA OF PLANE FIGURES:
- Trapezium = ½ × (Sum of parallel sides) × h.
- Rhombus = ½ × d1 × d2.
- General Quadrilateral = ½ × d × (h1 + h2).
◆ SURFACE AREA (3D):
- Cuboid = 2(lb + bh + hl).
- Cube = 6a².
- Cylinder = 2πr(r + h).
◆ VOLUME (3D):
- Cuboid = l × b × h.
- Cube = a³.
- Cylinder = πr²h.
◆ UNITS: 1 cm³ = 1 mL, 1000 cm³ = 1 Litre, 1 m³ = 1000 Litres.
◆ CONCEPT: Volume is the space occupied, capacity is the amount it can hold.

8.title: Ch 10: Exponents and Powers
8.content:
◆ NEGATIVE EXPONENTS: a⁻ᵐ = 1/aᵐ.
◆ LAWS OF EXPONENTS:
1. aᵐ × aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ.
2. aᵐ ÷ aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ.
3. (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ.
4. aᵐ × bᵐ = (ab)ᵐ.
5. (a/b)ᵐ = aᵐ/bᵐ.
6. a⁰ = 1.
◆ STANDARD FORM: Expressing very large/small numbers as k × 10ⁿ (where 1 ≤ k < 10).
- 0.000007 = 7 × 10⁻⁶.
- 560000 = 5.6 × 10⁵.
◆ SCIENTIFIC NOTATION: Used by scientists to handle cosmic or microscopic measurements.

8.title: Ch 11: Direct and Inverse Proportions
8.content:
◆ DIRECT PROPORTION: If x increases, y also increases in a way that x/y remains constant (k).
- Example: More pens bought → more cost.
- Formula: x₁/y₁ = x₂/y₂.
◆ INVERSE PROPORTION: If x increases, y decreases so that x × y remains constant (k).
- Example: More workers → less time to finish a job.
- Formula: x₁y₁ = x₂y₂.
◆ APPLICATIONS: Used in maps (scale), recipes, speed-time-distance, and bank interests.

8.title: Ch 12: Factorisation
8.content:
◆ CONCEPT: Writing an expression as a product of its factors.
◆ METHODS:
1. COMMON FACTORS: 2x + 4 = 2(x + 2).
2. REGROUPING: Arrange terms to find common brackets.
3. USING IDENTITIES: a² - b² = (a+b)(a-b).
4. SPLITTING MIDDLE TERM: For ax² + bx + c, find numbers whose sum is b and product is ac.
◆ DIVISION: Divide monomials by dividing coefficients and subtracting powers.
◆ POLYNOMIAL DIVISION: (Polynomial) / (Monomial) → divide each term separately.
◆ ERROR FINDING: Common mistakes like (x-3)² = x² - 9 (False, middle term 6x is missing).

8.title: Ch 13: Introduction to Graphs
8.content:
◆ PURPOSE: Visual representation of data to show trends.
◆ LINE GRAPH: Shows data that changes continuously over time.
◆ COORDINATES: (x, y) represent a point.
- X-axis (Horizontal): Abscissa.
- Y-axis (Vertical): Ordinate.
◆ ORIGIN: The point (0, 0) where axes cross.
◆ LINEAR GRAPH: A graph that is a whole unbroken line.
◆ INDEPENDENT VARIABLE: Plotted on x-axis (e.g., Time).
◆ DEPENDENT VARIABLE: Plotted on y-axis (e.g., Distance).
◆ SCALE: Choosing a proper unit representation for large numbers.

9.title: Ch 1: Number Systems
9.content:
◆ RATIONALS: Terminating or non-terminating recurring decimals.
◆ IRRATIONALS: Non-terminating non-recurring decimals (√2, √3, π).
◆ REAL NUMBERS: Every point on the number line corresponds to a unique real number.
◆ REPRESENTATION: Using Successive Magnification to locate numbers like 3.765.
◆ RATIONALISATION: Converting the denominator to a rational number by multiplying with the conjugate.
- Conjugate of (a+√b) is (a-√b).
◆ LAWS OF EXPONENTS: Extended to real number bases and rational exponents.

9.title: Ch 2: Polynomials
9.content:
◆ DEGREE: The highest power of x. Linear (1), Quadratic (2), Cubic (3).
◆ ZEROES: Roots of the polynomial equation p(x) = 0.
◆ REMAINDER THEOREM: p(x) divided by (x-a) gives remainder p(a).
◆ FACTOR THEOREM: If p(a) = 0, then (x-a) is a factor.
◆ IDENTITIES:
- (x+y+z)² = x²+y²+z²+2xy+2yz+2zx.
- (x+y)³ = x³+y³+3xy(x+y).
- x³+y³+z³-3xyz = (x+y+z)(x²+y²+z²-xy-yz-zx).

9.title: Ch 3: Coordinate Geometry
9.content:
◆ CARTESIAN SYSTEM: Perpendicular axes dividing plane into 4 Quadrants.
◆ QUADRANTS: I (+,+), II (-,+), III (-,-), IV (+,-).
◆ PLOTTING: To plot (3, -2), move 3 units right on x and 2 units down on y.
◆ AXIS POINTS: Any point on x-axis has y=0 (x, 0). Any point on y-axis has x=0 (0, y).
◆ ORIGIN: Point where x and y axes meet (0,0).

9.title: Ch 4: Linear Equations in Two Variables
9.content:
◆ FORM: ax + by + c = 0.
◆ SOLUTIONS: A linear equation in two variables has INFINITE solutions.
◆ GRAPH: Every linear equation in two variables represents a straight line.
◆ SPECIAL LINES: x = a is a line parallel to y-axis. y = b is a line parallel to x-axis.
◆ ORIGIN LINE: y = mx always passes through the origin (0,0).

9.title: Ch 5: Euclid's Geometry
9.content:
◆ DEFINITIONS: Point, Line, Plane (as defined by Euclid).
◆ AXIOMS: Common notions like 'Things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another'.
◆ POSTULATES: Specifically for geometry (e.g., All right angles are equal).
◆ FIFTH POSTULATE: The famous parallel postulate.
◆ THEOREM: Two distinct lines cannot have more than one point in common.

9.title: Ch 6: Lines and Angles
9.content:
◆ BASIC TERMS: Ray, Segment, Collinear points.
◆ ANGLES: Acute (<90°), Right (90°), Obtuse (>90°), Reflex (>180°).
◆ LINEAR PAIR: Sum of adjacent angles on a straight line is 180°.
◆ VERTICALLY OPPOSITE: When lines intersect, opposite angles are equal.
◆ PARALLEL LINES: Alternate interior angles are equal; Corresponding angles are equal.
◆ INTERIOR ANGLES: Consecutive interior angles are supplementary (sum = 180°).

9.title: Ch 7: Triangles
9.content:
◆ CONGRUENCE: Same shape and same size (≅).
◆ CRITERIA: SAS, ASA, SSS, RHS, AAS.
◆ ISOSCELES PROPERTIES: Angles opposite to equal sides are equal.
◆ INEQUALITIES: Side opposite to larger angle is longer. Sum of two sides > third side.
◆ CPCT: Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are equal.

9.title: Ch 8: Quadrilaterals
9.content:
◆ ANGLE SUM: Sum of 4 angles is 360°.
◆ PARALLELOGRAM: Diagonals divide it into two congruent triangles.
◆ PROPERTIES: Opp sides equal, opp angles equal, diagonals bisect.
◆ MID-POINT THEOREM: Line joining mid-points of two sides is parallel to 3rd side and half of it.
◆ CONVERSE: Line through mid-point of one side parallel to 2nd side bisects 3rd side.

9.title: Ch 9: Circles
9.content:
◆ CHORDS: Equal chords subtend equal angles at the center.
◆ PERPENDICULAR: From center to chord bisects the chord.
◆ RADIUS: Equal chords are equidistant from the center.
◆ CIRCLE THEOREM: Angle at center is double the angle at circumference by same arc.
◆ CYCLIC QUADRILATERAL: Sum of opposite angles is 180°.

9.title: Ch 10: Heron's Formula
9.content:
◆ AREA: Used for triangles when height is not known.
◆ SEMI-PERIMETER: s = (a+b+c)/2.
◆ FORMULA: Area = √[s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)].
◆ USAGE: Effective for scalene triangles and for finding area of quadrilaterals by splitting into triangles.

9.title: Ch 11: Surface Areas and Volumes
9.content:
◆ CONE: Slant height l = √(r²+h²). CSA = πrl. TSA = πr(l+r). Vol = 1/3 πr²h.
◆ SPHERE: SA = 4πr². Vol = 4/3 πr³.
◆ HEMISPHERE: CSA = 2πr². TSA = 3πr². Vol = 2/3 πr³.
◆ DENSITY: Mass = Volume × Density.

9.title: Ch 12: Statistics
9.content:
◆ DATA PRESENTATION: Frequency Distribution Tables.
◆ BAR GRAPH: For categorical data.
◆ HISTOGRAM: For continuous class intervals.
◆ FREQUENCY POLYGON: Joining mid-points of histogram bars.
◆ MEASURES OF CENTRAL TENDENCY: Mean (Average), Median (Middle), Mode (Most frequent).
